Day 4 - Learning ThinkPHP from Scratch: MySQL Data Basics, Part 1 (CRUD)

Last time we learned the basics of working with tables. This time we'll focus on the data inside those tables — how to operate on the actual rows. But before we get there, there are a few concepts worth getting straight first, because they'll make everything that follows a lot easier to understand.
First, let's talk about what CRUD is, because pretty much everything we do from here on out comes back to these four words. Each letter stands for one thing:

Update and Delete are the two you want to be careful with — especially Delete. I've said this a bunch of times already, so I won't labour the point. Anyway, let's go through how to add data, read data, update a table, and the scariest one of all: delete data.
The technically correct term here is "inserting a row," but personally I'm in the habit of saying "adding data." Still, it's worth knowing that the proper name is
we built in the last post as the example here. First we want to add a row — the height and weight of a classmate, 王小明. It looks like this:
language-sql shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
INSERT INTO hw_table (name, height, weight) VALUES ('王小明', 177.6, 70.5);
Same as before, let's paste the SQL into phpMyAdmin and run it:
Once it's done, we can head back to the table and check whether the insert worked:
But you'll notice something off. The weight we just entered had a decimal point, so why did 70.5 get rounded up to 71?
The reason is that back when we created the table in the last post, we set the
column to INT (integer), and an integer rounds off the decimal:
So we can put the column-modifying trick from the last post to use and fix this. Just run the following command:
ALTER TABLE hw_table CHANGE COLUMN weight weight float;
And now you can see the structure has been changed to
Now let's insert a fresh row and see if it works this time:
INSERT INTO hw_table (name, height, weight) VALUES ('李小美', 152.6, 69.5);
And there we go — the column takes the decimal just fine now:
Next up, updating a row. Let's say 李小美 has been training like crazy lately and her weight dropped from 69.5 to 45.6 (slimmed down!). The update syntax would look like this:
UPDATE hw_table SET weight='45.6' WHERE name='李小美';
After we run it, you can see 李小美's weight column has been updated:
to identify the row, but since this is just an example I'm using
. In the real world you'll run into people with the same name, and I'll cover
in more detail in a later chapter.
Last one is deleting data. Say today we want to delete the classmate 王小明. The syntax goes like this:
as the condition here. That's because if you delete using
, you could end up waving goodbye to everyone who shares that name.
Let's check the result and see whether 王小明 really got deleted:
Now let's test out the problem I just mentioned — using

as the condition and deliberately making that scenario happen. First, let's insert two students with the exact same name but different heights and weights. While we're at it, here's how to insert multiple rows at once:
INSERT INTO hw_table (name, height, weight) VALUES ('王小明', 177.6, 70.5),('王小明', 156.6, 47.5);
If all goes well, we'll see two people both named 王小明 but with different heights and weights:
Now let's run the delete, using
as the condition, and see what happens:
DELETE FROM hw_table WHERE name='王小明';
Once you run it, you'll find that every student named
So the takeaway here is: whether you're deleting or updating, when you use a condition, always use an absolute value (a key). Otherwise, tragedy awaits.
As usual, let's round up the SQL commands we covered in this post:

Last time we learned the basics of working with tables. This time we'll dig into the data inside those tables — how to insert, read, update and delete rows. But before we touch any of that, there are a couple of concepts worth understanding first, because they'll make every action that comes later click into place.
